case: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973)
summary: Established the Basic Structure Doctrine — Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution. This is the most important case in Indian constitutional law.

case: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
summary: Held that the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 cannot be suspended even during emergency. The procedure established by law must be fair, just and reasonable. Expanded Article 21 beyond narrow interpretation.

case: Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980)
summary: Held that Parliament cannot destroy the basic features of the Constitution through amendments. The harmony between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles is a basic feature.

case: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras (1950)
summary: First major constitutional case. Held that Article 21 was a standalone provision and not linked to other articles. Overruled by Maneka Gandhi.

case: Shankari Prasad v. Union of India (1951)
summary: Held that Parliament has power to amend Fundamental Rights under Article 368. This view was later overruled by Kesavananda Bharati.

case: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994)
summary: Held that secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution. President's rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review. The dismissal of state governments can be challenged in court.

case: Indira Sawhney v. Union of India (1992)
summary: The Mandal Commission case. Upheld 27% reservation for OBCs but introduced the creamy layer exclusion. Held that reservation cannot exceed 50% and there should be no reservation in promotions.

case: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (1986)
summary: Introduced the concept of absolute liability for industries engaged in hazardous activities. Established the 'polluter pays' principle in Indian environmental law.

case: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997)
summary: Laid down guidelines for prevention of sexual harassment at workplace until legislation is enacted. These guidelines were later codified as the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace Act, 2013.

case: Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967)
summary: Held that Parliament cannot abridge or take away Fundamental Rights through constitutional amendments. Overruled by Kesavananda Bharati which distinguished between abrogation and amendment.

case: Indian Express Newspapers v. Union of India (1985)
summary: Established that freedom of the press is part of Article 19(1)(a). Customs duty on newsprint was held to be a restraint on press freedom.

case: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal (1997)
summary: Laid down 11 guidelines to prevent custodial violence, torture and death in police custody. These guidelines must be followed by all police authorities.

case: R.C. Cooper / Bank Nationalization case (1970)
summary: Struck down the nationalization of 14 major banks. Held that the right to compensation under Article 31 is justiciable.

case: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla (1976)
summary: During the Emergency, held that the right to life and personal liberty can be suspended. Overturned by the 44th Amendment. Also known as the Habeas Corpus case.

case: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018)
summary: Decriminalized homosexuality by striking down Section 377 IPC as unconstitutional. Held that sexual orientation is a facet of Article 21 and 14.

case: Shayara Bano v. Union of India (2017)
summary: Struck down the practice of Triple Talaq (instant divorce) as unconstitutional and violative of Article 14. Also known as the Triple Talaq case.

case: Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)
summary: Held that the right to privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21. The judgment established that privacy is intrinsic to life and liberty and is a constitutionally protected right.

case: Vineet Narain v. Union of India (1998)
summary: The Jain Hawala case. Established guidelines for independence of CBI and enforcement agencies. Led to the creation of the Lokpal and Lokayuktas.

case: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985)
summary: Held that the right to livelihood is part of Article 21. Pavement dwellers cannot be evicted without providing alternative accommodation.

case: Unni Krishnan v. State of A.P. (1993)
summary: Held that the right to education is a fundamental right under Article 21 up to the age of 14. Later codified as Article 21A by the 86th Amendment.

case: Mohini Jain v. State of Karnataka (1992)
summary: Held that the right to education is a fundamental right flowing from Article 21. Capitation fees were declared illegal.

case: L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India (1997)
summary: Held that the power of judicial review under Articles 32 and 226 is part of the basic structure. Tribunals cannot substitute High Courts.

case: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra (1961)
summary: Leading case on the defense of grave and sudden provocation in murder cases. The accused was a naval officer who killed his wife's lover.

case: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab (1980)
summary: Established the rarest of rare doctrine for death penalty. Death sentence may be imposed only in the rarest of rare cases when the alternative option is unquestionably foreclosed.

case: Mithu v. State of Punjab (1983)
summary: Struck down Section 303 IPC (mandatory death penalty for life convicts who commit murder) as unconstitutional. Held that mandatory death sentences violate Article 21.
